planting.note: Direct-sow fresh seed in fall as soon as it ripens, or in early spring in moist, fertile, deeply dug soil in full sun to partial shade; lovage seed loses viability quickly — always sow fresh seed and expect germination in the first spring after fall sowing, or within 2–3 weeks of spring sowing at cool soil temperatures.

directions: Levisticum officinale is a bold, statuesque perennial herb of the carrot family (Apiaceae) native to the mountains of southern Europe, producing deeply divided, dark, glossy-green leaves with a powerful, unmistakable flavor of intensified celery — a single large plant can supply a household with celery-like flavoring throughout the growing season from every part: leaves, stems, seeds, and roots. Direct-sow fresh seed in fall in moist, deeply dug, fertile, well-drained to moisture-retentive soil in full sun or light partial shade; seed viability drops sharply after the first year, so always sow the freshest seed available. Once established, lovage grows to 4–6 feet tall with hollow, celery-scented stems and large umbels of small, greenish-yellow flowers in early summer — plants should be cut back hard after flowering to encourage a second flush of tender, flavorful young foliage. Harvest young leaves and stems freely throughout the season for use in soups, stocks, stews, and salads where celery flavor is desired; seeds can be used as a spice and dried roots are used in traditional herbal preparations.

planting.note: Direct-sow fresh, ripe seed immediately in fall — sweet cicely requires a prolonged cold stratification period of at least 8–12 weeks to break dormancy and will not germinate reliably without it; seeds sown in fall will germinate the following spring, while seed stored over winter quickly becomes non-viable, so fall sowing of fresh seed is strongly preferred.

directions: Myrrhis odorata is one of the finest shade-tolerant culinary herbs available to the northern-hemisphere gardener, thriving in the dappled shade of woodland edges, hedgerows, and north-facing borders where few aromatic herbs will grow; the large, finely divided, fern-like, bright-green leaves emit a strong, sweet anise-licorice scent and flavor when crushed, making them prized in the kitchen for flavoring fruit desserts, salads, and herbal liqueurs. Sow fresh seed immediately in fall directly in moist, humus-rich, well-drained to moisture-retentive, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial to full shade; the seed has a hard coat and requires a prolonged cold period (at minimum 8–12 weeks) to germinate — stored seed rapidly loses viability, so fresh fall sowing is essential. Plants grow 2–4 feet tall and spread to form attractive, long-lived clumps with flat-topped, white umbel flowers in late spring followed by large, ridged, glossy-brown seeds; they will self-sow freely in suitable conditions once established. All parts are edible and anise-flavored: the leaves, young seeds (excellent chopped into salads), and sweet, parsnip-like roots; the sweetness of the leaves means they can be used to reduce sugar needed in recipes for rhubarb and tart fruits.
sources: Jekka McVicar's Jekka's Complete Herb Book

planting.note: Direct-sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; like most members of the carrot family with large seeds, alexanders germinates best after a cold period — sow in moist, fertile soil near the coast or in mild, maritime gardens where it naturally thrives.

directions: Smyrnium olusatrum, known as alexanders, is one of the oldest cultivated European pot herbs, widely grown by the Romans throughout their empire as a celery substitute and still naturalized along coastal cliffs, roadsides, and hedgebanks in mild, maritime regions of Europe from the Azores to the British Isles; it is a robust, biennial or short-lived perennial with bright, glossy, three-lobed leaves, hollow celery-scented stems, and distinctive rounded heads of small, greenish-yellow flowers followed by large, ridged, black seeds. Direct-sow fresh seed in fall in moist, fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade, particularly in mild coastal gardens where it performs best; it is frost-hardy but prefers mild, maritime climates and does not thrive in hot, continental summers. All parts of the plant were used in Roman and medieval cooking as a celery substitute before celery (Apium graveolens) was cultivated: the young shoots and leaves are eaten as a potherb, the blanched stems as a vegetable, the flower buds in salads, and the roots and seeds as flavoring — the flavor is a distinctive, aromatic blend of celery and black pepper. Alexanders largely fell from culinary use after the widespread adoption of cultivated celery in the 17th century but is undergoing a revival among foragers, chefs interested in historical European cuisines, and those growing heritage kitchen garden crops.
sources: Roger Phillips and Martyn Rix — Vegetables

planting.note: Direct-sow fresh seed immediately after harvest in fall — angelica seed is short-lived and loses viability rapidly in storage; sow in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil at the edges of woodland, in damp borders, or beside water features in partial shade, and allow to self-sow freely as the parent plant is monocarpic and dies after setting seed.

directions: Angelica sylvestris is a tall, statuesque biennial or short-lived monocarpic perennial native to moist woodland margins, riverbanks, and damp meadows throughout Europe and temperate Asia, closely related to garden angelica (A. archangelica) but more tolerant of shade and with a wilder, more ornamental character; it produces large, pinnate, bright-green leaves, hollow, purplish-tinged stems, and showy, rounded umbels of white to pink-flushed flowers in summer that are exceptionally attractive to a wide range of pollinators including native bees, hoverflies, and butterflies. Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained to moisture-retentive, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade of a woodland edge, damp border, or streamside planting; seed viability is very short-lived and must be sown as fresh as possible — refrigerator storage can extend viability for a few weeks. The plant dies after flowering and setting seed (monocarpic) but self-sows prolifically in suitable moist, partially shaded conditions, maintaining a garden colony once established; remove spent flower heads promptly if self-sowing is not desired. The ornamental purple-dark cultivar 'Purpurea' ('Vicar's Mead') has become popular in naturalistic garden design for its striking, deep-purple stems and foliage, particularly effective in damp, partly shaded wild gardens.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; lesser calamint thrives in dry, sunny conditions and poor, stony or chalky soils — rich, moist soil reduces the intensity of its minty-peppery fragrance and flowering abundance.

directions: Calamintha nepeta is a compact, bushy, aromatic perennial of the mint family (Lamiaceae) native to dry, sunny grasslands, rocky hillsides, and chalky banks throughout southern and central Europe, producing small, softly hairy, gray-green, rounded leaves with an exceptionally pleasant minty-peppery-oregano fragrance and an unusually long succession of tiny, two-lipped, white to pale-lilac flowers from July through September that make it one of the single best perennial plants for attracting native bees, bumblebees, and other pollinators. Direct-sow seeds in spring in very well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline, preferably chalky or stony soil in full sun; it is summer drought-tolerant once established, requiring minimal irrigation in well-drained soils, and will decline in rich, moist, or waterlogged conditions. Lesser calamint has an ancient culinary history in Mediterranean Europe: the leaves and flowering tops are used fresh or dried as a flavoring for roasted meats (particularly in Italian cuisine, where it is sometimes called nipitella and used with porcini mushrooms), as a tisane, and as an aromatic addition to salads. Shear plants by about one-third after the first main flush of bloom to maintain tidiness and encourage a second flowering period; plants self-sow moderately and naturalize well on dry banks, gravel paths, and in dry-stone wall crevices.

planting.note: Direct-sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, gritty or sandy, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial sun; Scotch lovage is a plant of coastal clifftops, rocky shorelines, and seaside meadows and performs best in exposed, well-drained, cool, northerly sites with good air circulation.

directions: Ligusticum scoticum, known as Scotch lovage or sea lovage, is a compact, hardy, aromatic perennial of the carrot family (Apiaceae) native to coastal cliff tops, rocky shores, and seaside grasslands of Scotland, northern England, Iceland, Scandinavia, and northeastern North America, where it grows in some of the most exposed and salt-swept maritime habitats imaginable; the glossy, dark-green, divided leaves have a strong, pleasant aroma and flavor closely resembling lovage (Levisticum officinale) and celery, and were eaten by coastal peoples as a potherb and flavoring herb long before cultivated celery was available. Direct-sow fresh seed in fall in well-drained, gritty or stony, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in a cool, sunny to partly shaded position; it is a cool-climate coastal plant that prefers excellent drainage and good air circulation and is not well-suited to hot, humid summers. Young leaves and stems can be harvested in spring and used as a celery-flavored potherb in soups and stews; historically important to the diet of remote coastal and island communities in Scotland and Scandinavia, where it was one of few flavoring herbs available and was also used as a green vegetable. Scotch lovage is rarely offered commercially but is a unique and culturally significant culinary herb for northern gardens and maritime heritage plantings.
